turns.content: I'm reviewing a colleague's editor implementation and I thought using String::split would be the fastest way to handle large insertions, but it seems too slow.

You are right that String::split creates many intermediate allocations which hurts performance. For a text editor you need something like Rope which supports O(log n) splits and concatenations without breaking the string into thousands of small chunks. The Rust rope crate implements this using a linked list of blocks where each block is a fixed-size array, so operations stay efficient even on massive documents.
So I shouldn't use String::split and should instead build my own structure or use the rope crate? How exactly do I handle insertions in that linked list of blocks?

You can either implement your own Rope struct following the block layout or just import the Rust rope crate and call its methods. When inserting you find the split point in O(log n) time, then create a new block if needed and splice the blocks together using pointer arithmetic on the linked list nodes.
That sounds complex compared to just slicing strings. Can you explain why this linked structure is actually better than a simple array of bytes for large edits?

Think of it like editing a book by cutting out pages and inserting new ones rather than rewriting the entire manuscript every time. A simple byte array forces you to shift memory positions on every change, but the rope lets you only update the few blocks around your edit point while leaving the rest untouched.
